prompt:
Our ops team writes runbooks in a YAML workflow DSL and I need the execution core in Go: package `flowrun`, implemented in this module (I'd put it in flowrun.go). gopkg.in/yaml.v3 is already pinned in go.mod/go.sum — this checkout is fresh, so `go mod download` first if the module cache is cold. The acceptance tests in flowrun_test.go are the contract; don't touch them, and don't touch go.mod/go.sum either.

One thing before anything else, because the last prototype got this wrong: this is NOT a DAG engine. We already have a build-graph executor for parallel fan-out elsewhere; this one is a single cursor walking an ordered task list, one task at a time, fully deterministic. No goroutines, no real HTTP, no exec, no time.Sleep — the only side effects are through injected handlers, and the only time source is an injected clock.

Document shape. A workflow is:
- `document:` header — `dsl` must be exactly "1.0", `namespace` and `name` must be non-empty strings. Reject anything else.
- optional top-level `timeout: { after: <ISO-8601 duration> }` — the budget for the whole run.
- `do:` — a non-empty ORDERED list. Each entry is a single-key map: the key is the task name (unique within the list), the value is the task body.

API I want: `LoadWorkflow([]byte) (*Workflow, error)` parses + validates, and `(*Workflow).Run(Options) (*Result, error)` executes. `Options` carries `Handlers map[string]Handler` with `type Handler func(args map[string]any) (any, error)`, `Clock` (interface with `Now() time.Time`; only needed when the document uses timeouts — a workflow without any timeout must run fine with a nil clock), and `MaxVisits int` (0 means default 50, see loop guard). `Result` has `Status string` ("completed" or "failed"), `Context map[string]any`, `Events []Event`, `Error *ErrorInfo`.

LoadWorkflow validation (clear errors that name the offender): header rules above; `do` non-empty; unique task names; each task has exactly ONE type key among call/set/switch/raise/emit; every `then:` — including switch case `then:`s — is "continue", "end", or the name of a task in this do-list; every `timeout.after` parses as a duration; a `raise` must carry `error.type`.

Task types:
- `call: <name>` with optional sibling `with: {...}` — look the name up in Options.Handlers and invoke it with the evaluated `with` map (missing `with` = empty args). The handler's return value is the task's result.
- `set: {...}` — evaluate the map; the evaluated map is the task's result.
- `switch:` — ordered list of single-key maps (case name -> `{when?, then}`). Walk cases in order: a case with a truthy `when` is chosen, a case WITHOUT `when` is a default and is always chosen when reached. Follow the chosen case's `then`. Nothing matches and no default: continue to the next task. A switch stores NO result.
- `raise: { error: { status, type, title, detail } }` — the run stops with Status "failed" and Result.Error set to exactly that ErrorInfo (fields: Status int, Type, Title, Detail string). Context and Events collected so far stay on the Result. Run returns the Result with a nil Go error — a raised workflow failure is data, not an engine failure.
- `emit: { event: { type, source?, data? } }` — append Event{Type, Source, Data} to Result.Events in execution order (data values are evaluated like set values). Stores NO result.

Every task result is stored in the data context under the task's name, and later expressions read it there (`${ .fetch.rows }`). Tasks that store no result must leave NO key behind.

Common task fields:
- `if:` — evaluate; when falsy, the task is SKIPPED: it does not run, stores nothing, and its `then:` is ignored (the cursor just moves to the next task in document order).
- `then:` — "continue" (default), "end" (stop the run, Status "completed"), or a task name to jump to. Jumps go FORWARD or BACKWARD.
- `timeout: { after: <duration> }` — per-task budget, see below.

Loop guard: every time the cursor lands on a task (skipped-by-if landings count too) increment that task's visit count; when a task's count exceeds MaxVisits, Run fails with a `*LoopError{Task, Visits}` Go error (so budget 3 reports Visits 4).

Expressions. A string VALUE is an expression only when it starts with `${` and ends with `}`; anything else is a literal (no interpolation into surrounding text in this engine — the data-flow layer owns that, out of scope here). Inside the braces, after trimming spaces, the grammar is exactly: a dot-path, optionally followed by one comparison. Dot-path: `.ident(.ident)*` where ident is `[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*`; it walks nested maps in the context; a missing key or a non-map in the middle yields nil (never an error). Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `>`, `<`, `>=`, `<=` against ONE literal: a double-quoted string (no escapes), an integer, a decimal like `2.5`, `true`, `false`, or `null`. `==`/`!=` unify int-ish and float types numerically, compare strings and bools directly, and `null` equals nil/missing. The ordering operators require both sides numeric — anything else is an error. Malformed expression text (bad operator, junk after the literal, no leading dot) makes Run return an error. Evaluation contexts: `set` values, `with` values and `emit` data are evaluated RECURSIVELY through nested maps and lists — any string anywhere that is exactly one `${...}` is replaced by its value, and a single-expression string yields the RAW TYPED value (an int stays an int), never a stringified version. `if:` and `when:` take an expression string or a bare literal and coerce via truthiness: false, nil/missing, numeric zero, empty string, empty map/list are falsy; everything else is truthy.

Timeouts, all on the injected clock: record the workflow start when Run begins. Before a task starts, if the document has a workflow budget and (now - start) has already consumed it — remaining <= 0 — Run fails with `*TimeoutError{Task: <that task>, Limit: 0}`. The task's effective limit is min(its own `timeout.after` if any, remaining workflow budget if any); after the task finishes, if it took strictly longer than its effective limit, Run fails with `*TimeoutError{Task, Limit: <the effective limit>}` — so the Limit field shows the CLAMPED value when the workflow budget was the binding constraint. Using exactly the limit is fine. Durations are ISO-8601: `PT` followed by hours/minutes/seconds components like PT30S, PT5M, PT1H, PT1M30S (integers only); anything else fails at load.

Engine-plane failures are typed Go errors from Run: `*TaskError{Task, Err}` for an unknown handler or a handler returning an error (it must wrap the cause so errors.Is finds it), `*LoopError`, `*TimeoutError` as above; expression errors are plain errors. Declared workflow failure (raise) is the only thing that uses Status "failed".

Done means `go test -race -timeout 30s ./...` is green.
